description: 21 May 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Life Jackets for Children and Young Persons Bill Water Safety New Zealand Glen Scanlon, Chief Executive Anna Tootil, Chairperson Recreation Aotearoa Sam Newton, Advocacy Manager Graeme Edgeler Katherine (Katie) McNabb NZMarine Industry Association of New Zealand Peter Busfield, Advocacy Consultant Social Justice Aotearoa Jackie Foster, Chief Executive Nick du Preez Max Marshall Navigation Safety Group – Harbourmasters Grant Nalder, Group Convenor Jet Boating New Zealand Inc Katherine (Katie) McNabb, Representative on behalf of National Safety Officer Paul Vernel Steven Strong Petition of Free Fares NZ: Stop the proposed public transport fare hikes Free Fares NZ Patrick O’Connor, Member – National Organising Team Antonio Salamat, Member – National Organising Team Frances Mounties, Member – National Organising Team Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Genesis Energy Limited Genesis Energy Limited Barbara Chapman, Chairperson Malcolm Johns, Chief Executive Matthew Osborne, Chief Corporate Affairs Officer and Company Secretary
descriptionSummary: Life Jackets for Children and Young Persons Bill. Water Safety New Zealand. Glen Scanlon, Chief Executive

chunks.text: committee's time, and we really-- and the way that you have engaged with the feedback through the process to get us to today. This is a great space for us to consider extending mandatory lifejacket wear to people of all ages, and for exploring how that requirement might apply when a vessel is underway. We think it's a very positive direction, and it reflects the strength and the evidence that you've been hearing.

As we said when we last appeared, this is a once in a generation opportunity to make a meaningful, lasting reduction in drowning deaths in Aotearoa. A huge difference to those whanau whose family members who currently don't come home. However, we are here again because like our partners in the sector, we believe this is the moment to go further, not incrementally, but decisively, to ensure that the final law is clear, practical, and effective as it can be.

As I've mentioned at the start, we are conscious of your time, but I'll hand over to Glenn just to expand on why this is such a critical moment, and why getting it right and getting the settings right will make a huge difference. Glenn, over to you. Kia ora koutou. Thanks, Anna. Look, we continue to advocate for that one simple, clear rule for all recreational craft because the data shows people just don't die on small boats.

A very simple analogy, we don't have different seat-- have different seat- Sorry, Glenn, you've broken up there... different seat- You've frozen... regulations for different vehicles, except for buses. So Glenn, you're frozen.

I think, Anna, you might need to take over. That's good. Sorry, that might be unfair on you. Maybe we will-- Glenn, are you-- Can you unfreeze? Maybe turn your video off. I can dial in if you want. Put him on speaker. Sorry. Yeah. Think hang on. Yeah, okay. Look, I think, Anna, what we might do- Yep... what we might do is see if we can do technology a different way.

We'll come back to you in a moment. We've got another submitter. Yep. I'll pause your time and start again from- Thank you... from seven and a half minutes or eight minutes I'll make it, to come back again. So we'll have another go, getting him in. Okay? Cool. Right. Who else have we got? We've got Recreation Aotearoa.
Okay. Right. Sam.

Testing audio, testing video. Yeah. Look, all good there. We will-- Sorry, we've just had to jump to you because the previous submitter, the technology froze on us. Yeah. But anyway, so we'll start you. We've got your submission. Take that as read, please. Thank you very much for making a further submission. And look forward to what you've got to say. I'll give you a call with four to go.

And time starts now. Excellent. Thank you, Mr. Chairperson and members of the committee. I'm Sam Newton, advocacy manager for Recreation Aotearoa. Recreation Aotearoa is the professional association and industry body responsible for providing leadership, advocacy, guidance, quality assurance, technical advice, and professional development opportunities for those involved in the broader recreation sector.

We are the collective voice of recreation, ensuring that the value of recreation is recognized across government, industry, and communities. Our membership is broad, over 5,000 members. A lot of policymakers, people who work for councils, territorial local authorities, voluntary organizations, NGOs, regional sports trusts, a lot of outdoor recreation providers, and many others engaged in delivering facilities, recreation infrastructure, programs, and services that make recreation accessible for New Zealanders.

Relevant to this bill, many of our members work in aquatic facilities, either privately owned or council owned. So they're at the frontline of aquatic education. And many outdoor education providers who take young people and adults, generally on a nonprofit basis, out into the great outdoors and in many cases, on the water.

We thank you and your committee for the opportunity to speak again. We want to begin by commending your decision to consider an out of scope amendment to extend the mandate of lifejackets to all occupants. We regard this as a bold, evidence-based move. And we-- it recognizes the reality of water safety in New Zealand that perhaps the initial member's bill didn't quite land. And as someone who's worked in Parliament and has appeared before many select committees, I just want to commend the committee and say that feels

like democracy and Parliament working really well, that you went that extra half mile to make this work in a better way. So really appreciate that. You would have heard these statistics before in the first round, and you'll hear them again. Seventeen boating fatalities per year.

Average age of these victims is not a child. It's very much middle-aged white males like me. While the original bill focused on children under 15, we know they're not the primary risk group. We heard a lot of heart-wrenching evidence as I listened into those select committee hearings in the first rounds.

We know that in a tough situation, adults on board of a boat are unable to access or don their jackets once they're on the water, and they've tragically drowned in those highly stressful, highly complexOn occasions. By extending this requirement to all ages, you're addressing the core of the problem and, as the previous submitter began to mention, creating the biggest drowning prevention opportunity in a generation.

We strongly support the proposal to mandate wearing whenever a vessel is underway. Most incidents happen while boats are moving, leaving no time to grab a life jacket that is just merely stowed or carried on board. To ensure that this rule is practical and enforceable, we recommend a very clear definition of what underway is, and it needs to include the idea that you're moving through water and excluding times when a vessel is stationary, such as when it's moored to a buoy, anchored, or when occupants are sleeping or resting, for example.

While we celebrate the move toward protecting all ages, we have a couple of concerns regarding the proposed six-meter waterline length threshold. This limit remains a barrier to achieving a truly effective safety culture for a couple of reasons. Firstly, vehicle length is not a proxy for risk.

While smaller craft capsize more easily, fatalities are frequent on larger vessels. I'm sure you've been provided statistics from the Safer Boating Forum and the Coastguard that 93 people drowned on recreational vessels over 66 meters in the last 25 years. And at least 20, 33% of recorded influence incidents involved vessels eight meters or longer.

Drowning doesn't stop at an arbitrary six-meter line. Acknowledge there needs to be a line somewhere, but perhaps six meters is not it. Secondly, waterline length is quite a confusing metric. I'm a boat owner myself. I know that mine is a Kiwi craft and I know it's 15 feet long, but I'm sure that's not the waterline length.

I'm a fairly typical average recreational boater. I don't know the waterline length of mine. Inevitably different from the advertised model size, which was 15 feet. So it's sort of a unnecessary complexity to a rule that needs to be simple to be followed.

So, in summary, we need one clear simple national rule and the amendment, sorry, the members bill, the original members bill was a really good effort at getting after that. I really appreciate that. Your out of scope amendments is even better getting after that. But the rule needs to be, if the boat is underway, the lifejacket is on.

We don't ask drivers to decide which passengers are at risk enough to wear a seatbelt in a car, and we shouldn't expect skippers of a recreational boat to navigate a complex patchwork of regional bylaws and length-based thresholds understanding the waterline length of their boat. We have a history of doing well on this stuff.

Clear legislation works. The pool fencing laws in the late '80s, early '90s, I think they were, were a really good example of that. Very clear, very simple, widely adopted, widely understood, and widely accepted, and they reduced drownings materially. To summarize, we, Recreation Aotearoa urges the committee to proceed with the amendment to include all occupants as this is the most single effective way to save the estimated 12 lives lost per year that the Safer Boating Forum and the Coastguard will provide those stats.

Secondly, adopt the underway requirement with a plain language definition. And thirdly, to think about removing the six-meter threshold to ensure the rule applies to all recreational craft, providing the clarity and consistency New Zealanders deserve. We'd like to see wearing a lifejacket as being as normal as wearing a seatbelt.

One minute, Sam. Happy to take any questions. I'm sure you've received the data from numerous submitters and, I guess, to acknowledge that we've drawn upon perspectives of not only our own members who work in this realm, but our friends and colleagues and stakeholders in the Safer Boating Forum, Auckland Drowning Prevention, Coastguard, Water Safety. It's been a really great team effort.

Yeah. No, look, thanks, Sam. Simon, question. Thanks. Hi, Sam. Simon Thort here. Look, I was at the Auckland Boat Show on the weekend. I think it'd be fair to say that amongst the tens of millions of dollars' worth of beautiful boats that were for sale, and all of the people kicking the tires on trailers and boats and outboards and all the gear, when I asked people, "Would you need to wear a lifejacket on this vessel if it was underway?"

the manufacturers, the suppliers said, "Look, it's pretty safe. Unless it was really rough, why would you?" That's the prevailing view that I pick up from recreational boaties. So a law change is one thing. How do you effect a culture change without risking a massive backlash?

I'm surprised by that observation. I recreationally use a boat. I don't see a lot of people not using lifejackets when the boat's underway. Lifejackets are unobtrusive now. I'm sure you saw at the boat show, unobtrusive, inflatable lifejackets, fairly low cost.

There's numerous efforts to get that out and about. I think Coastguard and Water Safety, Maritime New Zealand do a great job, and the harbor masters in every place and space will benefit from-Consistency. Perhaps people have grown up and developed their boating skills in different places where the bylaws have been different.

But universalisation, consistency will, I think, address at least some of those observations you made. Yeah. Look, I am concerned because you've suggested that you think everyone wears a life jacket, whereas I've observed something different on the water in Auckland, where we don't have the same rules, say, as the Bay of Plenty.

And I'm interested in how you think we could effect a culture change to go along with the law change. Or do you think it's already done? I think there's numerous examples in New Zealand in the realm of drink driving, ACC, smoking prevention, where governments have made subtle and not so subtle interventions around publicity and education efforts.

I think the Coast Guard, Maritime New Zealand, and Water Safety are more than capable of making those efforts, if they are given the resourcing required. Okay. Thank you. Okay, thanks. Other questions? Yes. Celia. Please. And thank you for coming in, Sam, online. I wondered what you thought about, I think it was Fish and Game, but it might have been a different submission, that talked about when boats are drifting, not needing life jackets.

Yeah. Where do we draw the line between underway and at anchor, and where would you put the drifting side of things? Actually, Sam, if I can just add to that. They actually specifically talk about the very slow speed trolling at about two kilometers. Yeah, I think it must be less than five knots. Yeah, that sort of capacity. I mean, sort of walking speed. Yeah.

Look, I'm not an expert in that. Our members are not representative of that community and have no particular input on that. Much like waterline length or length of vessel, a line's got to be drawn somewhere. I think there is law inside the maritime legislation around very small dive boats allowing for wetsuits instead of life jackets.

So I'd interrogate that section of legislation. There might be something in there to draw inspiration upon. Thank you. Yeah, even my kayak goes faster than 2K. Yeah. As does my 2K row. Mine's doing similar. But, I appreciate the question. I've worked in parliament.

I know law is tricky. There's always stuff at the margins. There's always little things that need to be tidied up. But the general thrust of this is excellent, and the fact that you've gone for that out of scope amendment to go for a broader range of ages, to universalize it across all age groups is a good start, so.

Sam, I'm going to need to cut you-- I'm sorry. Great exposition, but I'm going to have to cut you off just time-wise to let people come on. But really appreciate it. Thank you, sir. Thank you very much. Okay, have we got Water Safety back? Yep. Okay, let's- So Glen's going to lead his camera. Okay. Right.

Glen and Anna, are you back? Yeah, they just came on. Oh. Right. Welcome back, Anna. Welcome. Right. Okay. I think, Glen, you're going to speak to us rather than leave your camera on. We'll see how we go. I'll leave my camera off again, just like I did last time.

Okay, right. Now that you know what I look like. So look, I'll pick up where I left off. We continue to advocate for one simple, clear rule for all recreational craft users, because the data shows, as the previous speakers said, people just don't die on small boats. A simple analogy and one you'll be getting used to is we don't have different seatbelt rules for different sized cars.

Indeed, more children have died on larger vessels. This is about saving lives. Unashamedly, we want the best result for that and creating behavior change, which Simon was just asking about. Look, in our October submission, we did refer to Tasmania and Victoria. I had a chat to our Tasmanian friends yesterday, and they've had their rules in place now for 20 plus years.

They say putting on a life jacket is now treated as second nature. It is as akin to wearing a seatbelt, and they've seen an 80% reduction in deaths per craft numbers since bringing that rule in, which is a fantastic result. Our modeling shows a clear rule for all recreational users will save 12 lives every year if they wear a life jacket.

And this year, just to finish so we can get to questions pretty quickly, seven of our 41 drownings so far, and we are having a tough year so far, have been from craft. Six, from memory, of those seven were not wearing a life jacket. So a clear rule for all recreational users, a practical one, could be saving lives as soon as we can get it into practice.

And finally, I do want to thank the committee and commend it for the amendment that has already been made. It's a great step. So we're open to take some questions. Okay. Shane. Thank you, Glen. Shane Reti speaking. I'd like to just pose a question that my colleague asked a previous submitter, because I think it's a useful one, about vessels underway, and the proposition from another submitter was that for inland lakes, those that are underway exclude less than two knots, those who are drifting or electronically stationary.

Do you have any view on that? Oh, look, we could probably take a look at some data. We have it, but I suppose the risk is you start creating carve-outs for different environments, which actually makes the rules harder to understand and implement. Yeah. And our view is that keeping the rules simple is the best approach.

As soon as you start splitting it up to oceans, lakes, rivers, et cetera, all of our water environments, it becomes much more difficult for people to understand and follow. Yeah. Thank you. And life jackets versus personal flotation devices, any issues with the bill's recommendation to change the semantics on that?

Oh, look, the big thing for us, to be honest, is that the law says that safety equipment needs to be used. We're very happy to talk to the officials about the technicalities of the bill as it progresses and the terminology. I suppose, as a consumer and a member of the public, as an audience for this material in the past, the sort of common vernacular for a lot of New Zealanders is life jacket, but we've got a very open mind.

Thank you. Okay, cool. Other questions? Still got a little bit of time. Look, I'm going to ask one. You've given us some stats on drownings on small watercraft and drownings on larger watercraft in terms of more or less than six meters in length. One thing that we haven't got in those stats is the circumstances which that-- I know that's an unfair question to say, do you know what happened to all these situations?

But do you have any idea how many of those, or roughly what proportion of those might have been in situations where a vessel's underway or not underway? Yeah, look, we- I can imagine there could be a lot of circumstances where some vessel was at anchor and somebody falls off it and drowns for whatever reason there might be. They might have been under the influence of something.

Yeah, but- So if we don't have anything, we may be making a judgment on a circumstance which we're not clear about. Yeah, look, that certainly happens, but the majority are definitely when vessels are underway. Look, we had a quick look at our data yesterday and our data team did a great job looking at the numbers very quickly.

And then in the cases where there is clear data, more than 90% of those or 95% of those were when vessels were underway. And you see from us and our sector colleagues, we're all very aligned around the underway piece. There will still be really tragic cases where people aren't, but the majority are.

So I guess the question then is, is there any pattern of what's going on with-- Because we're going to have to ask the question about scope with respect to if we've got a number of submissions, even if we were in mind to go down that track for beyond six meters, because that, again, as we've had to go with change of scope, for age, that would also bring into play a change of scope issue as well if we go beyond six meters, I would have thought.

We'll get some advice on that. But it would be useful to actually understand a little bit more about the circumstances of those drownings for vessels which are over six meters, particularly if it becomes quite large recreation vessels. Yeah, look, we'd have to dig into that. It's a very big data set, as you'll understand.

Yeah, no, I can understand that. Yeah. Yeah. So I can't give you an answer directly right now. Okay. Okay, shame. So I can just ask another. Is it true that the Australian legislation is 4.8? That is, that they do- Oh, it's different in different areas. So, from my understanding, Tasmania has a six-meter rule, quite similar to the one that's been proposed here, and other states have slightly different. So it's not actually common, which obviously it's an issue we want addressed here because we have that regionalization at the moment, which is confusing.

Thank you. Okay. I don't see any other submissions. So look, thank you very much. Thank you for bearing with the technology. I'm really appreciating the work you've done. And look, if there's anything else that you can give us in terms of data, that would be great, about those drownings on larger watercraft in particular. What actual circumstances we're talking about and what's going on there will be really useful.

We'll also ask that. Thanks. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Okay. Thank you. Thanks. Right. Okay. We have now got Graeme Eichler. Graeme, nice to see you. Always good to see you. Am I first time before this committee? Certainly in a while. And look, it might be, actually.

Yeah, I've probably done- I've seen you before other committees... once or twice before, but several terms ago. Okay. Now, sadly, we've only got you down for five minutes, but- That's okay I will give you a call with about two to go and- Yes away you go. Yep. Happy to take questions at any time. My submission is the one where I'm going to say the word life jackets once probably, and I'll probably be the only person, only time I say it.

You seem to have heard a lot of submissions and the fact that the submission period has been going on long has meant I've been able to read a large number of them and you seem to have the information you need to make some suggestions. My suggestion is not what you should do about the rules or the lengths and all those sorts of questions.

I think you've got the evidence you need and be able to probably get some more. It's if you are going to do something, how should you do it? And the simple fact is this isn't legislation. Mm. Or at least isn't primary legislation. This is something that cabinet could do in a few weeks if it wanted to. And that's the process that these types of things should go through.

These are rules like the driving rules and things like that, that go to Order in Council, and there's nothing in the bill as drafted that suggests it needs to be in a bill. And that is one of the potential solutions too. You've been discussing matters that might be out of scope. The bill as introduced was-Craft lengths of less than six meters. And the question, well, if we change that to eight meters or if we extend it to all pleasure craft or something, would that be out of scope?

I don't know the answer to that, but if your view is to look at this, form your view as to what the appropriate regulation should be, and at the end recommend, this bill does not need to pass, but here are the changes we would make to the rules. We ask the House to recommend them to the government.

Cabinet gets a paper a few weeks after you've finished your deliberations, and it goes to an order in council. And that would mean that you could make recommendations which are out of scope because it wouldn't be amending a bill that had been introduced. The normal process for changing these rules should be cabinet makes a decision and it goes through the executive council.

But there's absolutely a place for a committee to look at something like this. And, I've listened to some of your submissions previously and this morning. I've read a lot of them. And this is the type of thing that select committees do really well, and it's the type of thing that does not need to be connected to a bill. This is something, someone got lucky and had their bill drawn out of the biscuit tin. This is the type of thing the committee could have looked at.

Actually, we should hold an inquiry on that. And this would've been a great one, and you've got lucky in the sense that it was, in a sense, forced on you by decisions. But this sound like there has been some good discussions, but my simple point is this does not need to be legislation which passes through three more readings in the House.

I'm going to pick one quick question, which is, if we were to go down the track you're suggesting and a cabinet, whether it's this cabinet or another cabinet, whatever, decided they go, "Actually, no, we don't want to do that." They are the executive. We are reporting back to the Parliament. The Parliament may have a different view to the executive. Is there an issue there if the executive says no, and the Parliament said, "Well, actually, we disagree with you."

Is that constitutionally a- Yeah. That would be something that could happen and that would be the circumstances where I think if Parliament wants it, like the government has said, "No, we're not going to do it." Perhaps, future coalition negotiation reasons or whatever, Parliament could insist, and that would be the times when this sort of bill might be needed.

It's like- Okay. No, that's cool. I better do quickly for Simon and then Celia. And then Tracy. Thanks, Graeme. Look, helpful suggestion, but is it not the case that the original bill intended to reduce the risk of harm to children and young people on small vessels, now the committee's considering making this a much wider control over how people operate their vessels, whether they wear a life jacket or not.

Isn't it fair to say that while a government can change the rules, we also need to hear from all of the groups that have an interest in this. And, I was at the boat show on the weekend. Tens of thousands of people- Let's keep it quick, Simon Sure. There was tens of thousands of people there looking at buying boats and gear. I'm pretty sure that most of them would go out on their vessel on the weekend.

Question. Some of them would wear a life jacket, but not all of them. Isn't the whole purpose of having this type of inquiry in this committee to actually let everyone have a say? Absolutely. I think having this sort of inquiry is great. It's just the end result, my suggestion is, doesn't need to be a bill, it could be a rule instead, and the rule-making process could go through.

I'll take Celia. Quickly. Thank you, Graeme. Are there any other acts that you know of that only amend secondary legislation? And if you don't know now, maybe you could let us know. There would be acts certainly which repeal it. But as a general, I'm sure it's happened, but it would be exceedingly rare because-- Yep.

Thank you. Okay. That was exactly the- All good. Right. Graeme, always a pleasure. Thank you very much. Okay, now we've got Catherine McNair. Again, via-- Oh, look at that, perfect timing. Again, via video.

Catherine, welcome. Lovely to see you there. We've got you down for five minutes. You can take your submission as read, but if there's things you want to highlight, go for it. We better get you off mute. And I'll get your time started. I'll give you a call with two to go. Off mute.

Off mute. Off mute. Off mute, Catherine. You're ready. Yep, now you're good. Good morning, Mr. Foster and members of the committee. Good to be back so swiftly, and I absolutely commend the swiftness with which this has come through. As you can see from my submission, this has been a very personal thing in that I put a bill into the biscuit tin two years ago hoping to make this change to Maritime Transport Act 1994, rule part 91.4, and add a B in there rather than compulsory carriage of life jackets, that all

vessels six meters and under, PFDs in brackets life jacket, to be worn at all times while underway. Now, one of the questions that has come up several times, as you can see from the back of my submission, I've been doing a bit of teaching and stuff around boats for- Mm... five years or so, is the definition of underway, which simply means floating free. You are cast off.Making way is when you're actually steering a course.

Now, those are standard maritime definitions, so we don't need to get complicated about people drifting and drift fishing in lakes and stuff. They are making a course. They're floating free. If they are using any methods of steerage, making way, but once they are underway, in other words, floating free, they must have their life jackets on.

So, if you look through my concerns, one of the things has been is that as an educator and someone who moves around the countryside a lot, seven different life jacket rules, when carriage is the only one in the national legislation, it's been confusing, and confusion leads to poor compliance. So, at point seven, to have such inconsistency means that the legislation is falling short of the intention, making those who are on the water safe. In other words, undermining compliance by poor bylaw drafting. Okay.

So some are six meters and under, some are under six meters. So, the thing is, let's get it national. We know that that's the size of boat where people die most. It's not always by drowning. It can be hypothermia or any other number of events around a boat. We've had a lot of talk about drowning, not necessarily about drowning.

I want to draw your attention to the graph on page two of my submission, which is the stats. It's a graph of stats from the Tasmanian experience where they changed their legislation. It's self-evident what a huge difference it has made to make this one small change.

Mm. And then beneath that, at the last hearing as well, we had this discussion about life jackets and PFDs. Well, personal floatation, life jackets, all the same thing, but what I've done there is provide you a link into the Maritime New Zealand website, which shows you what is fit for what purpose.

Because they all have New Zealand standards, and it tells you there what's fit for what purpose. So really, apart from questions- We've got about a minute and a half left. Yeah, okay. Questions. Yeah. Yep. Questions. Fine. Okay. Okay. Questions, colleagues?

No, very clear. Very clear. Can I just ask one question? So you've also got attached the official information- Yes... and we've got particularly, which is useful for our consideration. Absolutely. Yeah. That is the whole list of all drownings, 2015 through to whatever the date is at the end.

Can we make that boating deaths, not drownings? Oh, sorry. Sorry, sorry, sorry. Yes. Okay. Boating deaths, please. Drownings associated with, but it is drowning associated with- Some will be drownings, but some will be other things. Yeah. Okay. But it gives us a bit of a feel for what happened then.

Yes, absolutely. And, I've worked as a safe boating advisor for Maritime New Zealand. I first came on board in 2004. So I've had a chance to watch all these various iterations of legislations and bylaws go through, and now is the time to make a change. Very simple, adding one little clause to an already existing bill.

Thank you, Catherine. I think we are good, and we're almost at time anyway. So thank you very much. Really appreciate it. Thank you for all that you've done. You will be seeing me back for Jet Boating New Zealand in about an hour's time. Okay. We'll see you soon. Okay. Right. Okay. Now we've got, right- We don't have the next submitter, but we've got the New Zealand Marine Industry Association.

I thought I saw Roger Thompson flash up there. It might have been us saying that he wasn't there. Oh, not there. Okay. So who have we got next, then? New Zealand Marine Industry Association. Right. Okay. Right. We'll need to accept the request. And that's that. Right. Are we still waiting for them to- Yeah, he's accepted now.
Yes. Good. Right. Okay, so.

Right. Who have we got there? Right. Peter. Yes. Good morning. Peter Busfield. I'm advocacy consultant to the New Zealand Marine Industry Association. Peter, I'll just tell you, we've got you down for 10 minutes. It's a very short submission, so if you don't need 10 minutes, that's great.

Gives time for others. That's fine. But I'll give you a call if you're still going at four, for questions. So away you go. Sure. Sure. And we'll keep it simple. So I'll just reiterate some key points. And personally, I have been part of the Safer Boating Forum for the 25 years it's been in existence, chaired by Maritime New Zealand, so have had some experience in boating safety.

In fact, it's part of our mission statement of the Marine Industry Association to promote safe boating. We have 520 members that design, build, and store boats. So our members have a close interrelationship with the users of boats, the customers of the marine industry. We support a bill thatRequires all people on a vessel under six meters underway to have to wear a life jacket.

Background to some of those points. 2 million people go boating every year. It's by far the largest recreational activity of New Zealanders, more so than football, walking, jogging, rugby, anything else. More people go boating than any other recreational activity. So there's 2 million people that go boating every year.

Unfortunately, there seems to be a historic average of 17 fatalities a year, which is not nice. But putting into perspective, over the years, the authorities, including ourselves, have promoted safe boating, and by and large, most boaties have a culture of safe boating.

So I don't think we have a huge problem in the system. In fact, through that good promotion of Maritime New Zealand, Coastguard New Zealand, ourselves, NZ Marine, Sports Fishing Council, that's all the fishing clubs, Yachting New Zealand, all the sailing clubs, Water Safety New Zealand, Jet boating New Zealand, Drowning Prevention, the Kayak Association, and the list goes on, have linked up together for the last 20 years, decided what the key safety messages are, and that has come across well to the community and has been accepted.

And that is demonstrated by the high percentage of recreational boats that carry life jackets on board for every person. It's over the 90%. It never used to be like that.
